title: The Virgin Mary, St. Benedict of Nursia, St. Serapion of Thmuis & St. Beryllus of Catania
narrative:
title": "The Virgin Mary, St. Benedict of Nursia, St. Serapion of Thmuis & St. Beryllus of Catania

narrative": "The Ever-Virgin Mary Theotokos, St. Benedict of Nursia, St. Serapion of Thmuis, and St. Beryllus of Catania

On the twenty-first of Megabit, the monthly commemoration of the most holy Theotokos gathers around it three witnesses whose lives illuminate the Lenten dimensions of her own witness — the ordered Rule that sustains the long fast, the pastoral theologian who guards the truth of the faith she bore, and the bishop at the far western edge of Sicily who honours the same mystery in the most geographically remote setting. The commemoration of the Ever-Virgin Mary in the midst of the Great Fast is the Ethiopian tradition's way of insisting that the Lenten discipline is not a matter of grim self-punishment but of tender maternal formation: it is the Mother who holds the community's Lenten intention before the throne of her Son, who intercedes for those who are failing the fast, and who draws the faithful forward toward the Paschal joy that she above all others knows awaits them at the end of the week of suffering.

Saint Benedict of Nursia returns in the Megabit cycle in the context of the Virgin's feast to recall the Benedictine tradition's particular Marian devotion — the monastery's daily singing of the Salve Regina, the Marian chapter at the end of each day's office, the consecration of the entire community's work and prayer to the one who bore the Word into the world. But more fundamentally, the pairing of Benedict with Mary in the Lenten Synaxarium reflects the tradition's understanding that the Rule of Saint Benedict is itself a Marian document in its deepest structure: a rule of listening, of receptivity, of the total availability to the divine will that found its supreme human expression in Mary's Fiat at the Annunciation.

Saint Serapion of Thmuis is this day's figure of pastoral theology under fire: the bishop of Thmuis in the Egyptian delta who was a close associate and correspondent of Athanasius during the Arian crisis, and who wrote a celebrated letter to Athanasius about the new heresy of the Pneumatomachians — those who accepted the Nicene confession about the Son but denied the full divinity of the Holy Spirit. Serapion's letter prompted Athanasius to write his own treatise on the Spirit, and the two works together constituted the first systematic defence of the full Trinitarian confession that would be completed at the Council of Constantinople. His Euchologion, a collection of liturgical prayers, is among the earliest surviving sources for the Egyptian liturgy and preserves eucharistic prayers of great beauty.

Saint Beryllus of Catania is this day's figure of apostolic transmission to the western Mediterranean: traditionally identified as the first bishop of Catania in Sicily, appointed by the Apostle Peter himself, Beryllus represents the apostolic foundation of the Western churches in their most geographically remote outposts. Sicily, the crossroads of the Mediterranean, received the Gospel in the first apostolic generation, and its earliest bishop is honoured in the Megabit Synaxarium as a witness to the completeness of the apostolic mission, which did not stop at Rome but continued to the edges of the known world. Mary whose fiat made the mission possible, Benedict who ruled the community that sustained it, Serapion who defended its doctrinal foundation, Beryllus who planted it at the end of the earth: four aspects of the one saving mystery. Amen.
